metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `applier.py` to the Scope section and specify that the re-imports of `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER` and `_AdfModule_Applier` (applier.py:110-130, __all__ at :322/:326) must be removed or replaced when those symbols are deleted from `inbound_translate.py`.
evidence: applier.py:110-130 imports _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate
applier.py:322 lists '_ADF_KEY_APPLIER' in __all__; applier.py:326 lists '_AdfModule_Applier'
Scope section lists only: _backend.py, outbound_comments.py, inbound_translate.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, inbound_fields.py, test files — applier.py is absent
Plan asserts 'the literal key at inbound_translate.py:315 is deleted' without accounting for applier.py's re-import of that same key
findings.finding: applier.py re-imports _ADF_KEY_APPLIER and _AdfModule_Applier from inbound_translate (applier.py:110-130, listed in __all__ at applier.py:322/326), but applier.py is excluded from the plan's Scope section. Deleting _ADF_KEY_APPLIER from inbound_translate.py without updating applier.py's re-import block will cause an ImportError breaking the entire applier module.
scenarios: After the plan's changes land: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import _ADF_KEY_APPLIER` in applier.py raises ImportError because the symbol was deleted — breaking applier, reconcile.py's getattr dispatch, and every test that imports applier.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py (remove re-imports of _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier from inbound_translate; remove from __all__)' to the Scope section, and add a corresponding testing note that the applier module imports cleanly after the change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `applier.py` to the plan's Scope and specify how its re-imports of `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module`, and `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate.py` are updated or removed when those symbols change.
evidence: applier.py:110-130: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import (_ADF_KEY_APPLIER, ..., _AdfModule_Applier, ..., _load_adf_module, _normalize_adf_body, ...)`
applier.py:__all__ lists `"_ADF_KEY_APPLIER"` (line 322), `"_AdfModule_Applier"` (line 326), `"_load_adf_module"` (line 361), `"_normalize_adf_body"` (line 367)`
Plan Scope section lists only: `_backend.py`, `outbound_comments.py`, `inbound_translate.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, `inbound_fields.py`, test files — `applier.py` is absent
findings.finding: `applier.py` imports and re-exports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module`, and `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate.py` (lines 110-130, `__all__` lines 322, 326, 361, 367), but `applier.py` is absent from the plan's Scope. Deleting `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER` from `inbound_translate.py` and replacing `_normalize_adf_body` with the port-routed call will break `applier.py`'s import block and leave its `__all__` listing stale symbols.
scenarios: After deleting `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER` from `inbound_translate.py`, `applier.py:111` raises `ImportError` at load time.
After replacing `_normalize_adf_body` with the port-mediated call, `applier.py:125` raises `ImportError` and the `__all__` list references a symbol that no longer exists in the module.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `applier.py` to the Scope. After rerouting `inbound_translate.py`, update `applier.py`'s import block to either (a) drop `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier` from its re-export list (no external callers were found for these), or (b) replace `_normalize_adf_body` with the new port-mediated call and propagate via `__all__` accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `apply_inbound_records.py` to the plan's Scope and specify how its three call sites for `_normalize_adf_body` are updated (e.g., routed through the backend's `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` or kept as a non-vendor utility).
evidence: apply_inbound_records.py:35: `_normalize_adf_body,` (imported from inbound_translate)
apply_inbound_records.py:145: `"description": _normalize_adf_body(fields.get("description"))`,
apply_inbound_records.py:268: `body_text = _normalize_adf_body(jc.get("body"))`,
apply_inbound_records.py:308: `"description": lambda v: _normalize_adf_body(v) if isinstance(v, dict) else v`
Plan Scope does not list `apply_inbound_records.py`
findings.finding: `apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` directly from `inbound_translate.py` (line 35) and calls it at lines 145, 268, and 308, but `apply_inbound_records.py` is absent from the plan's Scope. When the plan replaces `_normalize_adf_body` with the port-mediated `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` call, this direct import will break.
scenarios: If `_normalize_adf_body` is removed or renamed in `inbound_translate.py`, `apply_inbound_records.py:35` raises `ImportError` at load time, breaking the entire inbound apply path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach how the ADF-dict→text decode step in `_normalize_comment_body` is preserved after `_ADF_KEY` is removed from `outbound_comments.py`: either (a) widen `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment`'s signature to `Any` and have the Jira implementation ADF-decode before truncating, or (b) have `outbound_comments.py` call `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text` for the ADF-decode step and `backend.sanitizer.sanitize_comment` for the truncation step — and specify which is chosen.
evidence: Plan: 'FieldSanitizer ... gains the comment-fit surface the outbound comment path actually needs (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py)'
_backend.py:119: `def sanitize_comment(self, body: str) -> str: ...` — str-typed input, no ADF dict handling
jira_fields._sanitize_comment delegates only to `_truncate_comment_body` — no ADF decode path
outbound_comments.py:133-134: `if isinstance(body, dict): text = _load_adf().adf_to_text(body)` — ADF decode is required for inbound Jira comment bodies on the diff path
_diff_comments line 306: `raw = c.get('body', '')` for Jira comments, which are returned as ADF dicts by Jira Cloud v3
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for removing `_ADF_KEY` from `outbound_comments.py` is mechanically under-specified: `_normalize_comment_body` must ADF-decode raw Jira dict bodies (the `isinstance(body, dict)` branch calling `adf_to_text`), but the plan routes everything through `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str`, whose existing signature and implementation (`jira_fields._sanitize_comment`) only truncates strings. The plan claims FieldSanitizer 'gains the comment-fit surface ... currently provided by comment_limits.py AND adf.py', implying the new implementation absorbs ADF decoding — but does not specify whether the Protocol signature widens to `Any`, or whether ADF decode is delegated to `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` with `outbound_comments.py` calling both roles. If neither change is made, deleting `_ADF_KEY` breaks the ADF-body branch of `_diff_comments` for Jira (a raw `dict` body from `jira_comments` would crash `str.replace` or bypass normalization entirely).
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer comment-fit surface); outbound_comments.py:116-137 (_normalize_comment_body); _backend.py:119 (sanitize_comment signature)
scenarios: When `_diff_comments` processes a live Jira comment whose `body` is an ADF dict (Jira Cloud v3 API), and `_normalize_comment_body` no longer calls `adf_to_text` (because `_ADF_KEY` was deleted and `sanitize_comment(body: str)` only truncates strings), the dict comparison against `jira_bodies: set[str]` either crashes on `set.add(dict)` (unhashable) or never matches a local string body — reproducing the bug-85a1 duplicate-push regression.
A non-Jira backend (the epic's goal) with plain-string comment bodies would work correctly, but Jira itself would regress on the exact bug class this plan claims to preserve.
findings.suggested_fix: The cleanest decomposition is (b): `outbound_comments.py`'s `_normalize_comment_body` calls `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text(body)` (which returns plain text for any input, including ADF dicts) and then `backend.sanitizer.sanitize_comment(text)` for truncation. This keeps the Protocol signatures str-in/str-out, maps each responsibility to its correct existing role (InboundMapper decodes remote payloads; FieldSanitizer enforces send-side limits), and requires `outbound_comments.py` to resolve `backend` once and use both roles — consistent with the plan's own ADR 0035 principle that InboundMapper owns vendor→local payload translation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Approach whether `_normalize_adf_body` in `inbound_translate.py` is retained as a backend-routing shim (forwarding to `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text`) or deleted, and if deleted, add `apply_inbound_records.py` and `applier.py` to the Scope with their migration plan.
evidence: Plan Scope lists: `inbound_translate.py` — but not `apply_inbound_records.py` or `applier.py`
apply_inbound_records.py:35: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import ... _normalize_adf_body`
apply_inbound_records.py:145: `'description': _normalize_adf_body(fields.get('description'))`
applier.py:110-125: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import ... _normalize_adf_body`
Plan Approach: 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text... inbound_translate.py calls the backend's inbound mapper; the literal key at inbound_translate.py:315 is deleted'
findings.finding: The plan's Scope omits `apply_inbound_records.py` and `applier.py`, which both import and call `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate` directly (4 call sites in apply_inbound_records.py, 1 in applier.py). The plan routes `inbound_translate._normalize_adf_body` through `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` and deletes the `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER` literal from `inbound_translate.py`, but if `_normalize_adf_body` itself is re-implemented to call `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text`, the two consuming modules' imports (`from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import _normalize_adf_body`) need no source change — ONLY if `_normalize_adf_body` is retained as a thin wrapper. The plan does not clarify whether `_normalize_adf_body` is retained as a compatibility shim (forwarding to the backend) or deleted, which determines whether those callers need explicit migration.
scenarios: If `_normalize_adf_body` is deleted from `inbound_translate.py` (not retained as a shim), `apply_inbound_records.py` and `applier.py` break at import time — the bug-1bb2-5da5 defense-in-depth on the inbound create/update path is lost for all call sites except the one in `inbound_translate.py` itself.

If `_normalize_adf_body` is retained as a shim forwarding to `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text`, those callers work but still require a backend to be resolved — which may require additional wiring not currently in scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand AC2) requiring at least one test to invoke the `outbound_comments` path with a Jira-configured registry (e.g. using the real `select_backend(load_config())` call or an integration fixture) so that the registry-seam wiring is exercised, not only the sanitizer's output.
evidence: "Happy path: an outbound comment body formats and truncates byte-identically to today via the sanitizer route" — does not require the registry path to be traversed

"route via the configured backend's sanitizer via the registry seam (select_backend(load_config()) — pattern proven at outbound_differ.py:450-454)" — the wiring is described in Approach but not gated by any AC
AC2 says 'verified by the behavior tests in Testing' without specifying that those tests must go through registry resolution
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion or testing step explicitly verifies that the registry seam (`select_backend(load_config())`) in `outbound_comments.py` is exercised end-to-end — i.e., that the wiring from the neutral core path through the registry to the Jira sanitizer implementation is tested, not only that a directly-instantiated sanitizer produces correct output. A behavior test that constructs the sanitizer directly would satisfy AC2 without the new routing code ever running.
findings.location: Testing section (happy path bullet); Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: Unit tests pass by importing the Jira sanitizer directly and calling it, while `outbound_comments.py` has a broken `select_backend` call that would fail at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the happy-path Testing bullet: 'The happy-path test invokes `outbound_comments` through the public entry-point with a Jira-backed registry (using the existing test config fixture) so that `select_backend(load_config())` resolution is exercised end-to-end, not by constructing the sanitizer directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach whether `FieldSanitizer` gains a NEW method (e.g. `fit_comment(body: str, *, limit: int) -> str`) distinct from the existing `sanitize_comment`, or whether `sanitize_comment` itself is redefined to subsume fit-to-limit/truncation semantics; update `_backend.py` scope note accordingly.
evidence: "FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface the outbound comment path actually needs (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py)"
"sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str" — existing signature cited in the same sentence, but no statement of whether the new semantics extend this method or add a new one
findings.finding: The plan states FieldSanitizer 'gains the comment-fit surface' (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation) but never specifies whether this means adding a NEW method to the FieldSanitizer Protocol or expanding the contract of the already-pinned `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str`. The implementer cannot determine from the plan whether to add e.g. `fit_comment(body: str) -> str` alongside the existing method or to subsume truncation semantics into `sanitize_comment`. This ambiguity risks a misimplementation that satisfies no existing AC (both the outbound routing and the Jira adapter implementation depend on the correct shape).
scenarios: Implementer adds a new `fit_comment` method; plan consumer expects an expanded `sanitize_comment` → Jira adapter doesn't implement the right surface → runtime AttributeError or silent no-op truncation
Implementer expands `sanitize_comment`; other callers relying on the old lighter-weight semantics get unexpected truncation behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the FieldSanitizer paragraph: e.g. 'A new method `fit_comment(body: str) -> str` is added to the Protocol (distinct from `sanitize_comment`); `outbound_comments.py` calls `sanitizer.fit_comment(body)` at the two previously-lazy-loaded call sites.' Or alternatively state that `sanitize_comment` is expanded and name the callers that must tolerate the new semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all existing implementors of FieldSanitizer and InboundMapper (including test fakes/stubs) that must gain the new methods, and add an explicit back-out note (e.g., 'revert the _backend.py Protocol additions and both call-site changes atomically') so the rollback path is stated.
evidence: 'FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface' (Approach)
'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str' (Approach)
Scope lists only adapters/jira/backend.py and inbound_fields.py as files that 'implement the new members' — no enumeration of other implementors
ABSENCE: no rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or all-implementors audit mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan adds new required methods to two existing Protocol roles (FieldSanitizer gains a comment-fit/truncation surface; InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text) but only names the Jira adapter files in Scope as the implementors to update. Any other existing implementors — notably test doubles, fakes, or stubs used in the wider test suite — would silently break at typecheck or runtime. No explicit rollback/back-out plan is stated for the case where the new method signatures prove wrong or an overlooked implementor is found.
scenarios: A test fake for FieldSanitizer that doesn't implement the new method causes make typecheck to fail with no clear remediation path
A future second-backend stub that was started but not yet in scope is silently broken
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rollback note to the Approach: 'Back-out: revert _backend.py Protocol additions, the two call-site changes, and the Jira implementations atomically — no data is mutated so revert is safe at any point.' Also add a Scope bullet: 'Audit all FieldSanitizer and InboundMapper implementors (including test doubles in tests/) and update each with stub/no-op implementations of the new methods.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly whether the outbound comment-fit capability extends the existing `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` (with updated semantics) or adds a new named Protocol method (e.g. `fit_comment(body: str) -> str`), and give its exact name and signature in the Approach section.
evidence: "FieldSanitizer...gains the comment-fit surface the outbound comment path actually needs (fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adapters/jira/adf.py)"
Contrast: InboundMapper surface IS explicitly named: `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str`
Existing `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` is cited but it is unclear if its semantics are extended or a new method added
findings.finding: The new `FieldSanitizer` Protocol surface for the outbound comment-fit path is not given an explicit method name or signature. The plan says FieldSanitizer 'gains the comment-fit surface' but does not state whether this extends the existing `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` (changing its semantics) or adds a new Protocol method (e.g. `fit_comment`). An executor must resolve this design choice without guidance.
findings.location: Approach section, FieldSanitizer paragraph; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py scope bullet
scenarios: Executor extends `sanitize_comment` semantics but the Protocol's call sites in outbound_comments.py expect a distinct method, causing a mismatch.
Executor adds a new method but names it differently from what a future backend implementor would expect, creating Protocol drift.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the FieldSanitizer paragraph, e.g.: 'A new Protocol method `fit_comment(body: str) -> str` is added alongside `sanitize_comment`; the Jira implementation delegates to `comment_limits.truncate` + `adf.encode`.' Alternatively, if extending `sanitize_comment`: 'The semantics of `sanitize_comment` are widened to include fit-to-limit truncation with marker preservation; no new method is added.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `_backend.py` protocol docstrings (and AGENTS.md / design doc if one exists for the port roles) to document the new `FieldSanitizer` comment-fit + truncation contract and the new `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` signature, so future backend implementors have a canonical reference.
evidence: 'FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface' — new semantic contract added to existing role
'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str' — new required method on existing protocol
No mention of updating AGENTS.md, protocol docstring, or design doc anywhere in the plan
Rejected alternatives section justifies YAGNI for a new role but does not note where the extended contract is documented for implementors
findings.finding: The plan extends two existing protocol roles in `_backend.py` with new method contracts (`FieldSanitizer` gains comment-fit + marker-preserving truncation semantics beyond the existing `sanitize_comment`; `InboundMapper` gains `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str`) but does not mention updating AGENTS.md, inline protocol docstrings, or any design doc to reflect these new required interface members — leaving future backend implementors without a canonical contract reference.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py; Approach section (FieldSanitizer and InboundMapper paragraphs)
scenarios: A developer adds a third backend (e.g., Linear) and implements InboundMapper but misses normalize_rich_text because it isn't mentioned in the protocol doc; the sweep test won't catch this (it checks literal keys, not protocol completeness).
A future refactor of FieldSanitizer misunderstands its scope because the comment-fit semantics were never recorded alongside the existing sanitize_comment signature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring to each new method in `_backend.py` specifying the expected semantics (e.g., 'truncate body to backend byte limit preserving truncation marker; return sanitized string' for FieldSanitizer; 'decode vendor rich-text dicts to plain text; pass strings through unchanged' for normalize_rich_text). If an AGENTS.md or protocol inventory exists for the five port roles, add a one-liner entry for each new member there.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `apply_inbound_records.py` (3 calls to `_normalize_adf_body`) and `applier.py` are in scope for routing through `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text`; if not, document why those sites are exempt from the sweep gate and reconsider whether the new Protocol method meets Rule-of-Three (≥3 routed call sites) to justify adding it to the interface.
evidence: Plan: 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str'
apply_inbound_records.py:145: `'description': _normalize_adf_body(fields.get('description'))`
apply_inbound_records.py:268: `body_text = _normalize_adf_body(jc.get('body'))`
apply_inbound_records.py:308: `'description': lambda v: _normalize_adf_body(v) if isinstance(v, dict) else v`
applier.py:125: imports `_normalize_adf_body` from inbound_translate
Plan scope section does NOT list apply_inbound_records.py or applier.py
ABSENCE: no mention of routing the apply_inbound_records call sites through InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text
findings.finding: `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` is added to the Protocol for a single call site (inbound_translate.py:315), while 3+ existing call sites of the same function (`_normalize_adf_body`) in apply_inbound_records.py (:145, :268, :308) and applier.py remain outside this plan's scope — giving the new Protocol method only 1 routed consumer, which fails the Rule-of-Three bar for a new abstraction.
scenarios: After this plan lands, apply_inbound_records.py still directly calls _normalize_adf_body (imported from inbound_translate), which still lazy-loads adapters.jira.adf — the backend-neutrality goal is only partially achieved.

The neutrality sweep gate (which checks core modules for the literal 'rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira') would still trip on apply_inbound_records.py unless that file is also exempted or its import chain is not considered a 'package-root core module'.
A Protocol method with 1 call site is premature abstraction that adds interface surface without the Rule-of-Three justification.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) include apply_inbound_records.py and applier.py in this plan's scope so normalize_rich_text has ≥3 call sites and the sweep gate is comprehensive, or (b) drop normalize_rich_text from the InboundMapper Protocol and instead route inbound_translate.py:315 through the already-present `inbound_fields._normalize_jira_body` (which lives in the Jira adapter and will move with inbound_fields.py per 556a-5a1f), keeping the Protocol interface minimal.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
